blocks.text: Angela Mahoney
General Manager
Lopez, Mcbride and Evans
Dear Ms. Mahoney:

This letter confirms the terms on which Verdant Health Advisory LLC (the "Firm") will perform the Ambulatory Network Study for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. Please read it in full. Once you have signed and returned it, it governs the work in place of anything said in our earlier conversations.
Background and Objectives

Lopez, Mcbride and Evans has added ambulatory capacity faster than the reporting built to manage it, and the questions you put to us in January were, fairly summarized, three: what each service line in the network actually contributes, where cases are being placed and why, and whether the arrangements now in force would survive an informed reader who is looking for problems rather than for reassurance. Those are answerable questions. They are not quick ones, and the answers will depend more on the condition of your data than on any method of ours.
We will not tell you at this stage which conclusion the study will reach, and you should be wary of an adviser who would.
Scope of Work
The study comprises four workstreams.

items: Service line contribution. Restatement of ambulatory volume, revenue, and directly attributable cost onto a single definitional basis, so that a case counted at one facility means the same thing as a case counted at another.
Site of service and case placement. Analysis of where procedures are performed across the network, the drivers of that placement, and the margin consequences of the current pattern.
Capacity and utilization. Review of block allocation, room turnover, and scheduled against realized utilization at the ambulatory sites, measured against the capacity the network already holds.

Arrangement review. A documentary review of the physician arrangements supporting the ambulatory platform, including a fair market value assessment of compensation terms where the documentation supports one.

blocks.text: The fourth workstream carries a qualification worth stating at the outset rather than in a footnote at the end. Fair market value is a term of art. Where the underlying documentation is incomplete, we will report the gap rather than close it with an assumption, and the assessment will say which arrangements we were able to opine on and which we were not.
Out of Scope

The study is not an audit or any other attestation service, and nothing we deliver may be characterized as one. We will not render legal advice; where a question is properly counsel's, we will say so and stop. We will not value the entity or any interest in it, and we will not model transactions you have not asked us to model.
Approach and Deliverables

We work from your source systems rather than from summary extracts wherever you can give us access, and we reconcile each figure back to a raw input before it appears in anything we issue. Where a figure cannot be reproduced from source, it does not go in. Where your data runs out and an inference begins, the deliverable marks the seam.

You will receive a data request in the first week of fieldwork, an interim read delivered in person at the midpoint, and a final report with supporting exhibits. Findings will lead the report. Recommendations will follow in a separate section, labeled as recommendations, so that no one reading the document a year from now can quote a suggestion of ours as though it were a finding.
Engagement Team

Daniel Alvarado, Principal, will lead the engagement and is responsible for the arrangement review and the fair market value assessment. He is also your point of contact if anything about the work concerns you. Joshua Dougherty, Analyst, will build and own the models underlying the contribution and utilization analyses. Barbara Freeman, Analyst, will lead the data request, the site of service work, and the drafting. I review every deliverable before it leaves this office, as I do on all of the Firm's engagements.
We will not add or substitute team members without notice to you.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for the work described above is {{fact:f:E-2023-001.fee}}, inclusive of the deliverables listed and exclusive of travel, which we bill at cost and only with your prior approval. We invoice monthly against progress, and our invoices are payable within thirty days of receipt. If the scope changes, we will tell you before we do the work, in writing, with the fee effect stated. We do not bill for scope we did not agree with you in advance.
Term and Termination

Fieldwork begins {{fact:f:E-2023-001.start}}, and we have planned the study to run approximately seven months from that date, subject to the timeliness of the data we receive. Either of us may terminate this engagement on ten days' written notice. On termination you will owe fees for work performed through the termination date, and we will deliver our work product in whatever state it then stands, with its limitations described.
Confidentiality

We will treat non-public information you give us as confidential and use it only for this engagement. Access within the Firm is limited to the team named above. Patient-identifiable information is not required for this study and we ask that you not send it; if data containing it reaches us, we will notify you and handle it under a business associate agreement executed for that purpose. The obligation does not extend to information already public or to disclosure required by law, and we will give you notice of any such requirement where we may lawfully do so. You should assume we retain working papers for the period our professional obligations require.
Limitation of Liability

The Firm's aggregate liability arising out of this engagement, on any theory, is limited to the fees paid to us under it, except in the case of our fraud or willful misconduct, for which no limitation applies. Neither party is liable to the other for indirect, incidental, or consequential damages. Our deliverables are prepared for your internal use. They may be shared with your counsel and your auditors, who are not entitled to rely on them as their own work, and they may not otherwise be distributed or quoted without our written consent.
Governing Law

This engagement is governed by the laws of the state in which the Firm maintains its principal place of business, without regard to its conflict of laws provisions. We will attempt in good faith to resolve any dispute by negotiation between principals before either of us initiates proceedings.
If the foregoing reflects your understanding, please sign below and return one copy to my attention. We are glad to have been asked, and we are ready to begin.
Very truly yours,
To: Engagement team
From: Barbara Freeman
Re: Ambulatory Network Study, kickoff and sequencing

The Ambulatory Network Study for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}} is live; our start date of record is {{fact:f:E-2023-001.start}}. This memo is the working version of what we told the client we would do, restated for us, with the parts I think are actually hard flagged as such. Daniel Alvarado has seen the scope. The sequencing below is mine and I expect it to come back marked up.
Objectives

The engagement letter frames three questions: what each ambulatory service line contributes, where cases are being placed and why, and whether the physician arrangements supporting the platform hold up under a close reading. My read is that the first one is a definitional problem before it is an analytical problem. Lopez, Mcbride and Evans has grown by adding sites, and I would be surprised if a case at one site is counted the way a case is counted at another. Until we know that, any contribution figure we produce compares two things that are not the same thing. So the basis work goes first and everything else queues behind it.
Workstreams

items: W1, definitional basis and data intake. Inventory the source systems, map how volume, revenue, and directly attributable cost are captured at each site, and produce a single restated basis. Owner: Joshua Dougherty, with me on the site mapping. Nothing downstream starts until W1 has a basis Joshua will put his name to.
W2, site of service and case placement. Where procedures are performed across the network, what drives placement, and the margin consequence of the current pattern. Owner: me.

W3, capacity and utilization. Block allocation, turnover, and scheduled against realized utilization at the ambulatory sites. Owner: Joshua Dougherty. Depends on W1 for the case counts and not much else, so it can run in parallel once the basis holds.

W4, arrangement review and fair market value assessment. Owner: Daniel Alvarado. This runs on its own track and in its own vocabulary; per Daniel, nobody drafts in that register except him, and having read one of his memos I am not going to argue.

blocks.text: The second question is the one I keep turning over. We are being asked why cases land where they land, and the honest answer is that we do not yet know what evidence would settle it. If the referral and scheduling records carry the reason, we can show it. If they do not, we will be able to describe the pattern precisely and explain it only by inference, and I would rather find that out in the first month than in the sixth.
Client Interface

One process note. Angela Mahoney is our single point of contact, and every data request goes through her rather than to site staff directly, even when we happen to know who has the file. Angela has been direct about wanting to see what we ask for before her people spend a week on it. That is reasonable, and it protects us too: it puts a date on when we asked, which matters if the schedule later comes up for discussion.
Next Steps

items: Data request drafted by me, reviewed by Joshua Dougherty, then to Angela Mahoney. One comprehensive request, not a trickle of small ones.
Kickoff call with the client operations leads, scheduled through Angela Mahoney.
Joshua Dougherty opens the reconciliation workbook now and records each definitional mismatch as he finds it, rather than reconstructing the list at the end.
Daniel Alvarado requests the arrangement documents directly. Those are not going in the general data request.
Standing internal check-in weekly until the basis is settled, then every other week.

blocks.text: Last thing, a habit I want us keeping from the first week rather than adopting in month five: where the client's data runs out and one of us starts inferring, that seam gets marked in the working papers when it happens, not later when nobody remembers which was which.

Minutes of a working session on the Ambulatory Network Study for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. Recorded by Daniel Alvarado. These minutes record what was discussed and what was decided. They are not a transcript, and where a matter was left open they say so.
Attendees

items: Daniel Alvarado, Principal, Verdant Health Advisory
Barbara Freeman, Analyst, Verdant Health Advisory
Joshua Dougherty, Analyst, Verdant Health Advisory
Angela Mahoney, General Manager, Lopez, Mcbride and Evans

blocks.text: Discussion

Restated basis. Joshua Dougherty reported that the restatement of ambulatory volume onto a single definitional basis is complete at four of the network's sites and open at the fifth, where the scheduling system records a bilateral procedure as one case and the billing system records it as two. He declined to publish a network-level contribution figure until that is resolved, on the ground that he cannot at present reproduce the fifth site's case count from raw inputs. No one argued with him.

Case placement. Barbara Freeman presented the preliminary placement analysis and was careful to separate what the data shows from what it suggests. The pattern is visible. The explanation is not yet supported: the referral source field is unpopulated in a material share of records at two sites, and attributing placement to referral behavior today would be an inference standing where a finding ought to stand. Angela Mahoney asked whether the field could be reconstructed from the scheduling notes. Barbara Freeman said possibly, at a cost in hours she would rather quantify than guess at, and Angela Mahoney asked for the estimate before deciding.

Arrangement review. I reported that the arrangement documents received to date are sufficient to describe the population but not, in every case, to opine on it. Several executed statements of work reached us as scanned images with no text layer, which makes them legible to a reader and inert to a search. Angela Mahoney confirmed that the contracting office prints, signs, and scans as a matter of routine, and that no electronic originals exist for those documents. We agreed that the review proceeds by hand where that is so, and that the assessment will state which arrangements it covers and which it does not. I restated, as I did at the outset, that where documentation does not support an opinion we report the gap rather than close it.

Utilization. Joshua Dougherty noted that block allocation data are complete for every site, and that scheduled and realized utilization diverge at two of them by a margin large enough to be worth explaining. He was asked whether the divergence is a scheduling artifact or a real capacity finding. He said he does not know yet and that the distinction turns on how the sites record a cancellation, which he is checking against raw inputs before he will characterize it either way.

Timeline. Angela Mahoney asked whether the interim read remains on plan. It does. She asked separately whether the fifth site's case count threatens the final report. My answer was that it threatens the network-level contribution figure specifically, and that we would rather deliver that figure late and reproducible than on time and unsupported. She asked what she should tell her Board in the meantime. We agreed she should tell them the work is on plan and that no findings are available to report, which is accurate on both counts.
Action Items

rows: Reconcile the fifth site's case definition between the scheduling and billing systems
Estimate the hours to reconstruct the referral source field from scheduling notes
Decide whether to authorize the referral source reconstruction as a scope change
Provide the site contact for the scheduling system export
Complete the manual review of the scanned arrangement documents
Circulate the interim read structure for internal review

blocks.text: Status report on the Ambulatory Network Study for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. Prepared by Barbara Freeman, Analyst, and reviewed before issue.
Summary

The engagement is at its midpoint and substantially on plan. Three of the four workstreams are where we expected them to be. The exception is the restated contribution basis, which is complete for most of the network and open at one site, for a reason we understand and can correct. Nothing found to date changes our view that the study will answer the questions Lopez, Mcbride and Evans asked. Equally, nothing found to date supports a conclusion we would be willing to state in a report, and this document should not be read as previewing one.
Accomplishments This Period

Definitional basis. Joshua Dougherty has completed the restatement of ambulatory volume, revenue, and directly attributable cost onto a single basis at four of the network's five sites. That is the load-bearing piece of the study. Without it, comparing service line contribution across sites compares quantities that are not the same quantity. The fifth site remains open because its scheduling and billing systems disagree on how a bilateral procedure is counted, one case against two. The disagreement is now precisely characterized, which was most of the work; the correction itself is not difficult.

Case placement. The placement analysis is drafted across the network. It shows a consistent pattern in where procedures are performed, and the pattern is stable enough across the period examined that we do not expect it to move. I want to be careful about what that means. We can show the pattern. We cannot yet show why it exists, because the referral source field is unpopulated in a material share of records at two sites. Any explanation we offered today would be an inference, and it would be sitting in the place where a finding belongs. We have scoped the work required to close the gap; whether to do it is a decision for you, not for us.

Capacity and utilization. Block allocation and turnover data are in hand for all sites, and the utilization model is built and reconciling. Joshua Dougherty has it reproducing from raw inputs end to end, which is his standing condition for releasing a figure. Scheduled and realized utilization diverge at two sites by a margin worth explaining. Whether that is a real capacity finding or an artifact of how those sites record cancellations is exactly the kind of question we would rather answer before the interim read than assert during it.

Arrangement review. Daniel Alvarado has received and inventoried the arrangement documents. A subset arrived as scanned images with no text layer and is being reviewed by hand. That is slower than the alternative but it is not blocked, and it is on schedule as re-planned. He has been clear with us that the fair market value assessment will cover the arrangements the documentation reaches and no others, and that the assessment will name the exceptions rather than leave them to be noticed.
Upcoming Work

In the next period we will close the fifth site's case definition and publish the network-level contribution restatement; deliver the interim read in person, findings first with recommendations separated and labeled; complete the manual review of the scanned arrangements; and, if you elect it, begin the referral source reconstruction. The fair market value assessment will state explicitly which arrangements it covers and which ones the documentation does not support an opinion on. Daniel Alvarado will not extend it past what the record carries, and we would not ask him to.
Budget

The engagement fee is {{fact:f:E-2023-001.fee}}, fixed for the scope set out in the engagement letter. Hours consumed to date are close to proportional to elapsed time, and we are not forecasting an overrun on the agreed scope. Two items would fall outside it: reconstructing the referral source field from scheduling notes, and any expansion of the arrangement review beyond the population inventoried. Neither has been performed, and neither will be without a written scope change with the fee effect stated in advance.
Risks

items: Fifth site case definition. Until scheduling and billing agree, the network-level contribution figure cannot be reproduced from source and will not be published. Mitigation is underway. The risk is to timing rather than to validity, and we would rather issue the figure late than issue one we cannot reproduce.

Referral source completeness. If the field is not reconstructed, the final report will describe the placement pattern and will not attribute a cause to it. That is a real limitation on the study's usefulness, and it is better decided with the limitation in view than discovered at delivery.

Scanned arrangement documents. The manual review is proceeding on schedule. The residual risk is that an arrangement for which no executed original can be located cannot be opined on, in which case the assessment will say so and name it.

Data latency. Two of our four extract requests this period returned later than agreed. No slippage has resulted. If the pattern holds through the next period it will consume float we would rather keep.

blocks.text: Minutes of a working session on the Ambulatory Network Study for {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. Recorded by Daniel Alvarado. As before, these are a record of discussion and decision rather than a transcript, and open matters are identified as open.
Attendees

items: Daniel Alvarado, Principal, Verdant Health Advisory
Barbara Freeman, Analyst, Verdant Health Advisory
Joshua Dougherty, Analyst, Verdant Health Advisory
Angela Mahoney, General Manager, Lopez, Mcbride and Evans

blocks.text: Discussion

Draft report. Barbara Freeman circulated the full draft ahead of the session and walked the structure: findings first, then a separate section of recommendations, labeled as such. Angela Mahoney raised the question we expected, which is whether the recommendations could move forward in the document for her Board's convenience. The answer was no, and the reason is not formatting. A recommendation printed among findings is quoted later as a finding. Angela Mahoney accepted the reasoning and asked instead for a one-page summary that preserves the separation. Barbara Freeman will draft it.

Contribution restatement. Joshua Dougherty confirmed that the fifth site's case definition is reconciled and that the network-level contribution figures now reproduce from raw inputs at every site. He walked the exhibit rather than narrating it, which was the efficient choice. Angela Mahoney asked whether the ranking of service lines by contribution is sensitive to the cost allocation method. Joshua Dougherty said the top and the bottom of the ranking are not, the middle is, and that he has put the sensitivity in the appendix rather than the body so that it does not read as a hedge on the findings that hold.

Fair market value assessment. I reported that the assessment is complete for the arrangements the documentation supports and identifies by name the two that it does not reach, in both cases because no executed original could be located and the scanned copy in the file is unsigned. Angela Mahoney asked whether we could rely on the contracting office's confirmation that those two were executed. My answer was that we can report the confirmation as a representation of management, attributed to management, and that we cannot convert it into an opinion of ours. She did not press the point. Counsel will see the section before the Board does, which is the right sequence.

Utilization. Joshua Dougherty closed out the divergence between scheduled and realized utilization at the two sites that showed it. The larger share is an artifact of how those sites record cancellations and the remainder is a real finding, and the exhibit now separates the two rather than reporting a single number that would have been half explanation and half accident.

Remaining work and limitations. Barbara Freeman noted that the placement analysis will be delivered with its limitation stated plainly: the referral source field was not reconstructed, so the report describes the pattern and does not attribute a cause to it. Angela Mahoney confirmed that this was her decision, taken on our estimate, and asked that the minutes reflect it. They do. She asked whether the limitation could be confined to the appendix. It cannot, and it will appear with the finding it qualifies.
Action Items

rows: Draft the one-page summary preserving the findings and recommendations separation
Move the cost allocation sensitivity to the appendix and cross-reference it from the findings
Provide a management representation covering the two unlocated executed originals
Route the fair market value section to client counsel
Complete principal review of the full draft and route it for firm review
